text: The patient is a sixty-one years old male. Year and a half ago, the left side pneumonectomy was performed for the central lung cancer. Patient did not pass the regular examinations. Six weeks ago, he began to notice weakness, fever and sweating.

Lung cancer progression was excluded by the chest CT, brain MRI and bone scan. During the abdominal CT, the tumor in the liver was found. We performed biopsy and primary hepatocellular cancer was diagnosed pathologically.
AFP was normal. C hepatitis test was positive, but the patient did not have clinical and instrumental signs of chronic liver disease decompensation.

In the chest cavity, post pneumonectomy state is observed with the displacement of the mediastinum to the left side. During the arterial and venous phase of CT scan, we can see the huge tumor in the second, third segments of the left liver and partially in the segment IVB.
The average size of tumor is ninety five to seventy five millimeters. And after the preoperative evaluation of the patient, we decided to perform the left hepatectomy.
The intraoperative localization of the tumor coincided with the radiological picture.

The surgeon begins the procedure by intersecting the left lobe ligaments to increase mobility. The tissues above the inferior vena cava are transected to locate the trifurcation. The most medial vein in this trifurcation is identified as the left hepatic vein, which is carefully dissected and encircled, noting its proximity to the middle hepatic vein inflow.
The surgeon fully visualizes the left hepatic vein, encircles it, and prepares for ligation, with the division planned for the end of the operation following the intersection of the liver parenchyma.

The surgeon proceeds to dissect the liver hilum structures, employing anatomical liver resection techniques. Two strategies are considered: dividing the Glisson's pedicle or visualizing and dissecting the structures of the hepatoduodenal ligament to separately divide vessels and ducts.

The surgeon mobilizes the neck of the gallbladder, revealing the common hepatic duct. The division of the cystic artery exposes the wall of the right hepatic artery, serving as a landmark. Dissection continues on the left side of the hepatoduodenal ligament, visualizing the common hepatic artery and progressing towards the gate of the liver.
During the dissection, several arterial branches are identified that potentially supply the left liver and segment one. The surgeon delays their division until the anatomy is clearly defined.
The surgeon mobilizes the neck of the gallbladder and divides the cystic artery, a branch of the right hepatic artery. The wall of the right hepatic artery is visible beneath the common bile duct.
The surgeon divides the gallbladder neck and performs a cholecystectomy. The arterial branches supplying the left liver are carefully dissected, with the largest vessel being encircled for ligation.
The surgeon dissects fibrotic tissues to expose the left portal vein, which is then encircled for further manipulation.
The surgeon dissects tissues to visualize and encircle the left bile duct. The left portal vein is ligated and divided.

The surgeon divides the largest arterial branch supplying the left liver. The anatomy of the liver gate is highlighted, showing the hepatic bile duct with its left and right branches. The right hepatic artery, a branch of the common hepatic artery, is visible beneath the common bile duct. The arterial supply to the left liver is non-standard, with three potential branches identified, one of which is already ligated.

The surgeon observes the stump of the left portal vein and divides the left bile duct. The line of demarcation, marking the boundary between the right and left livers, is visible. The standard resection line begins at the gallbladder bed and extends to the inferior vena cava, reaching the middle hepatic vein on the anterior surface of the liver.
The surgeon draws the classic Rue Cantlie's line on the liver, marking the anatomical boundary for right or left side hepatectomy following the line of demarcation.

The surgeon begins the dissection of the liver parenchyma using an ultrasound scalpel for superficial division. As the dissection progresses deeper, small vessels and ducts are clipped and ligated carefully.
The surgeon carefully dissects the hepatic parenchyma to locate the middle hepatic vein, meticulously dividing its left branches to minimize blood loss.

With a significant portion of the liver parenchyma already divided, the surgeon returns to the liver gate to evaluate the arterial blood supply of the left lobe. Both previously undivided arterial vessels are now visible and directed towards the left liver, allowing for their safe ligation.

The surgeon completes the transection of the liver parenchyma, safely dividing the left hepatic vein. The left hepatectomy is performed, removing segments two, three, and four while preserving segment one. The final view shows the anatomical left lobe resection with the remnant right liver, segment I, left hepatic vein stump, medial hepatic vein, and the gate of the right liver.
The surgeon displays the resected liver specimen, which is identified as having primary hepatocellular carcinoma.
